text: Here we present a case of transanal total mesorectal excision using near infrared light to assist in the join up between the abdominal and transanal components. The patient was a thirty seven year old male referred from a secondary centre with a low rectal cancer about one to one point five centimetres above the anal rectal junction. MRI staging staged the tumour at T-to-N-zero, and the patient didn't have any neoadjuvant treatment.

The abdominal team uses a standard endo-eye laparoscope to continue the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, while the transanal team has dissected cranially past the pelvic floor using a fluorescence-enabled pinpoint laparoscope. The near-infrared mode is activated, revealing a signal in the anterior plane of dissection, with no visible white light due to the penetration properties of near-infrared light.

The abdominal team temporarily removes their light source from the abdomen, then reintroduces it, revealing a stronger near-infrared light signal on the pinpoint display as the abdominal and transanal teams approach the join-up point.
